journal-title-group.journal-title: International Journal of Veterinary Science and Medicine

publisher.publisher-name: Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Cairo University

title-group.article-title: Monocephalus dipygus in a 4-month-old mixed-breed puppy: clinical, radiographic, ultrasonographic, karyotyping and surgical intervention
title-group.alt-title: INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF VETERINARY SCIENCE AND MEDICINE

contrib-group.aff: Facultad de Ciencias Agropecuarias, Universidad Técnica de Machala, Machala, Ecuador

author-notes.corresp: CONTACT Mauro Nirchio Tursellino mauro.nirchio@gmail.com Facultad de Ciencias Agropecuarias, Universidad Técnica de Machala, Av. Panamericana km 5.5, Vía Pasaje, Machala070150, Ecuador

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Informa UK Limited, trading as Taylor & Francis Group.

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: Congenital anomalies are rare in veterinary medicine, and their aetiology, development, clinical presentation, and management remain poorly understood. This study documents a rare case of multiple congenital anomalies occurring simultaneously in a single individual. We present a comprehensive analysis of a 4-month-old mixed-breed puppy diagnosed with caudal duplication (monocephalus dipygus), integrating clinical, radiographic, ultrasonographic, cytogenetic findings, and surgical intervention. The puppy exhibited several external abnormalities, including a supernumerary limb, a single tail, two penises, and two distinct scrotal sacs. Radiographic examination revealed additional metatarsal and phalangeal bones in the supernumerary limb. Ultrasonography and exploratory laparotomy further identified two bladders, a single prostate, and bifurcated ureters, each connecting to a kidney. Cytogenetic analysis was conducted to investigate potential chromosomal abnormalities underlying these congenital anomalies, as we hypothesized that these malformations might be linked to chromosomal aberrations. The successful surgical removal of the supernumerary limb significantly improved the dog’s mobility. Notably, since each bladder was independently connected to a kidney, surgical intervention in the genitourinary system was avoided to prevent potential impairment of urinary function. This case highlights the complexity of congenital anomalies in canines and emphasizes the importance of a multidisciplinary approach to their diagnosis and management.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Congenital abnormalities can significantly jeopardize the survival and health of offspring [1]. The prevalence of congenital anomalies in animals is around 7% with 15% mortality rates [2–4]. These abnormalities result from deviations in normal anatomical or functional development during pregnancy, often leading to structural and functional irregularities that arise during embryogenesis and become evident shortly after birth. Congenital anomalies may manifest in varying degrees of severity, and their aetiology can encompass genetic disorders, chromosomal aberrations, and non-genetic or environmental factors, such as maternal health, nutrition, infections, exposure to pollutants, heavy metals, toxins, medications, and mechanical complications during gestation [3,5,6].

This report provides a comprehensive analysis of a rare case of caudal duplication (monocephalus dipygus) in a mixed-breed puppy, integrating morphological, radiographic, ultrasonographic, and surgical findings. The puppy exhibited unique external abnormalities that required detailed clinical evaluation, surgical intervention, and postoperative management. Our aim is to expand the limited veterinary literature on such rare congenital conditions by offering insights into the presentation, diagnosis, and management of this anomaly. Given the unusual presentation, we hypothesize that the observed congenital abnormalities may be linked to underlying chromosomal aberrations. Therefore, we performed a cytogenetic analysis to explore potential genetic causes, with the hope of contributing to a deeper understanding of such anomalies and informing future cases.

sec.title: Clinical evaluation
sec.p:
During the clinical examination, a mixed-breed dog was presented at the Veterinary Medical Specialties Clinic at the Universidad Técnica de Machala. The dog exhibited clear abnormalities in the pelvic region, including an additional pelvic limb (Figure 1a). Additionally, a urogenital anomaly was observed, with the presence of two penises (Figure 1a,b). The clinical evaluation included a thorough physical examination, assessing heart rate, respiratory rate, body temperature, capillary refill time, and pulse. A comprehensive blood profile was also conducted to assess the animal’s overall health Figure 1.
Anatomical abnormalities in the pelvic region of the dog. The image highlights the presence of an extra pelvic limb (a). Urogenital anomaly with the presence of two penises (a,c).

sec.title: Radiographic evaluation
sec.p: Radiographs were taken using a Univet 300 hF veterinary radiography system (Multimage S.R.L., Cavaria, Italy) at 30 kV and 100 mA.

sec.title: Ultrasonographic evaluation
sec.p: Ultrasounds were performed using a Chison Eco 2 portable ultrasound scanner (Xinwu District, Wuxi, Jiangsu, China) equipped with a 7.5 MHz microconvex transducer.

sec.title: Amputation of the accessory pelvic limb
sec.p: To address the intricate anatomy and improve the patient’s quality of life, a two-phase surgical approach was undertaken. In the initial stage, the additional pelvic limb was amputated by disarticulating the femur.

The surgical procedure began with premedication for tranquilization and preventive analgesia, using acepromazine maleate 1% (Inadrim™-Richmond, Vet Pharma) and 0.5 mg/kg morphine (Morphine Life Solution for Injection 10 mg/1 ml). Anaesthetic induction involved the administration of 3 mg/kg Propofol (Propovet™-Richmond, Vet Pharma) and 0.25 mg/kg Diazepam (Dazam™-Richmond, Vet Pharma). Anaesthesia was maintained through inhalational sevoflurane (Baxter) and a sacrococcygeal epidural block with 1 ml of 2% Lidocaine hydrochloride (Propovet™-Richmond, Vet Pharma) to provide targeted analgesia for the pelvic region.

Amputation of the accessory pelvic limb was performed via hip disarticulation, following the surgical techniques described by Weigel [7] and Fossum [8]. Bupivacaine was administered to the femoral and sciatic nerves to ensure effective analgesia. During manipulation, an increase in the dog’s heart rate indicated pain, prompting the immediate administration of additional anaesthetic before proceeding with the incision.

An incision was made over the insertion of the accessory limb, located on the left side of the tail. The skin and atrophied muscles of the medial thigh were dissected to expose a neurovascular bundle consisting of an artery, vein, nerve, and the coxofemoral joint. The blood vessels were carefully ligated, and the femoral head of the accessory limb was disarticulated. The remaining muscles and fascia were approximated using absorbable 3–0 polyglycolic acid sutures, and the skin was closed with simple interrupted sutures using 3–0 surgical nylon.

sec.title: Exploratory laparotomy
sec.p: With the patient in dorsal recumbency, a midline skin incision was first made from the umbilicus to the prepubic region, providing access for exploration of the kidneys, ureters, bladder, and prostate. Following this, a subcutaneous incision was performed to expose the external fascia of the rectus abdominis muscle and to locate the linea alba, while controlling any bleeding from small subcutaneous vessels. Next, the abdominal wall was elevated, and a precise incision was made along the linea alba using a scalpel to inspect the internal surface for possible adhesions. To extend the incision cranially and caudally, scissors were employed, ensuring it matched the length of the initial skin incision. During exploration, the falciform ligament, which obstructed access to cranial structures, was carefully released from its attachments to the abdominal wall. The cranial end of the ligament was ligated to ensure proper haemostasis before it was completely removed, allowing for a clearer view and easier access to the cranial abdominal structures.

sec.title: Cytogenetic analysis
sec.p: To investigate potential chromosomal alterations, a cytogenetic analysis was conducted following the procedure detailed by Guerrero et al. [9]. Three millilitres of blood were obtained via cephalic venipuncture using a syringe that was heparinized by drawing up heparin (5,000 IU/mL) and then expelling it until approximately 25 µL remained on the needle tip, providing an estimated total of 125 IU of anticoagulant to prevent clotting during sample collection. After one hour of sedimentation, the leukocyte layer was collected and seeded into 5 mL of PB-Max culture medium (Gibco), then incubated for 72 hours at 38°C. Subsequently, 75 µL of a 0.0125% colchicine solution was added to the culture 30 minutes before cell harvesting. Cells were collected by centrifugation at 1,500 rpm for 10 minutes. The cell pellet was then treated with a hypotonic solution of 0.075 M KCl, followed by fixation with Carnoy’s fixative (methanol: acetic acid, 3:1) repeated three times. The fixed cells were spread onto glass slides by dropping the cell suspension from a height of 10 cm, allowing them to air-dry.

Chromosome preparations were stained using a 5% Giemsa solution (Sigma) in phosphate buffer at pH 6.8. Metaphase cells were photographed using an Olympus B×53microscope (Olympus Corporation, Ishikawa, Japan) equipped with an Olympus DP73 digital camera coupled to cellSens Dimension software (Olympus). Chromosome counting was performed on the digital images using the “Count” tool of Photoshop (Adobe Systems, Inc., San José, CA, USA, Version 2015.0.0).

sec.title: Clinical evaluation
sec.p: The 4-month-old patient, weighing 4.5 kg, presented with a third pelvic limb and two penises (Figure 1). The supplementary limb was non-functional, smaller than the two normal pelvic limbs, and suspended in a flexible position. The animal experienced challenges with walking and showed signs of imbalance while running. Additionally, the left penis appeared smaller than the right (Figure 1c), and externally, two distinct scrotal sacs were observed, each containing a single testicle. Furthermore, urine discharge was evident from both penile urethras.

The recorded vital parameters comprised a heart rate of 140 beats per minute, a respiratory rate of 38 breaths per minute, and a temperature of 37.5°C. Additional indicators featured a capillary refill time of 2 seconds, a robust pulse, and a responsive reaction to external stimuli. The animal remained awake, alert, and receptive, displaying an appropriate level of activity and responding positively to the owner’s instructions. Although classified as 2.5 out of 5 for body condition, indicating moderate thinness, the dog exhibited relative stability throughout the examination.

The blood evaluation unveiled a haematocrit of 26%, indicating a reduced concentration of red blood cells and suggesting the presence of moderate anaemia. Furthermore, macrocytosis and anisocytosis were noted, signifying an increase in red cell size and variability, respectively. Hypochromasia, linked to insufficient haemoglobin levels (6.1 g/dL), was also evident. The confirmation of regenerative anaemia was established by the limited presence of reticulocytes. Conversely, the leukocyte response was 28.86%, displaying reactive lymphocytes and monocytes, implying a potential inflammatory or immunologic reaction, likely associated with a chronic infection. This prompted the administration of amoxycillin + clavulanic acid (15 mg/kg of animal weight) for a 7-day duration. To address the anaemic condition before the planned surgical intervention, the patient underwent deworming with pyrantel pamoate and Praziquantel. Additionally, a multivitamin containing B-complex, Iron, and Omega 3 was administered. A shift to a premium balanced diet was also recommended for dietary improvement.

sec.title: Radiological evaluation
sec.p:
The radiographs in Figure 2 depict two views of a dog with a supernumerary pelvic limb. In the ventrodorsal projection, two normal pelvic limbs are seen symmetrically aligned with the spine, alongside a third supernumerary limb originating from the left side of the pelvic region. This additional limb is positioned asymmetrically, anchored near the caudal aspect of the pelvis, particularly along the ischiatic region. It consists of a vestigial coxal bone, a well-defined femur, tibia, fibula, as well as metatarsal and phalangeal bones, indicating a degree of structural integrity, although its functionality remains limited. While the coxofemoral joints of the normal limbs display proper alignment and remain intact, the attachment of the third limb lacks a typical synovial articulation and connects abnormally to the pelvic bones through soft tissues and a vestigial coxal bone. The bones in this third limb are underdeveloped, showing signs of atrophy, likely due to disuse or an inadequate biomechanical connection. The lateral projection shows that the third pelvic limb is situated caudally on the left side and extends ventrally in a flexed position. This view clearly reveals the femur, tibia, fibula, tarsals, metatarsals, and phalanges. A distinct hip joint is absent, and the limb’s abnormal positioning indicates minimal to no functionality. The bones of the accessory limb are smaller and display reduced density compared to the primary limbs, reflecting limited functionality and signs of developmental atrophy. Figure 2.
Radiological projections of the patient: ventro-dorsal (a) and lateral (b) demonstrating the hip and the three pelvic limbs.

Figure 3 provides a detailed view of the accessory limb, showing both a clinical photograph and a dorsoplantar radiographic projection. The radiograph reveals seven distinct metatarsal bones with complete phalanges, indicating polydactyly. This arrangement suggests a duplication or malformation in the accessory limb, with each digit exhibiting its own well-formed skeletal structure. In the accompanying clinical photograph, the overlying skin and soft tissues appear unremarkable, with no visible signs of inflammation or infection. Figure 3.
(a) Dorsal view of the additional hand and (b) dorso-plantar radiological projection.

sec.title: Ultrasound evaluation
sec.p:
The ultrasonographic examination revealed the presence of two bladders with regular borders and anechoic content, consistent with bladder duplication (Figure 4a). Additionally, a single enlarged prostate gland (prostatomegaly) was identified, featuring irregular borders and slightly heterogeneous echotexture. The gland exhibited hyperechoic and symmetrical echogenicity, and urethral duplication was also noted (Figure 4b). Figure 4.
Ultrasound findings demonstrate (a) bladder duplication with two distinct bladders and regular borders; (b) an enlarged prostate with irregular borders and hyperechoic echogenicity (prostatomegaly); and (c) normal-sized kidneys with a hyperechoic renal cortex and clear corticomedullary differentiation.

Both kidneys appeared normal in size and structure. Since their ultrasonographic characteristics were identical, we present an image of one kidney for clarity and space optimization. The depicted kidney shows irregular borders, a hyperechoic renal cortex, well-defined corticomedullary differentiation, and a normal corticomedullary ratio (Figure 4c).

sec.title: Amputation of the accessory pelvic limb
sec.p:
Figure 5a illustrates the additional limb during pre-surgical preparation, providing insight into the extent of the anomaly. Subsequently, in Figure 5b, the patient is depicted post-surgery, with visible sutures indicating the completion of the surgical procedure. Finally, in Figure 6, the patient is shown fully recovered, with restored mobility and motion, affirming the effectiveness of the surgical intervention. Figure 5.
Pre-surgical preparation (a) displays the accessory pelvic limb located near the tail. Post-surgical recovery (b) shows the patient after amputation, with visible sutures. Figure 6.
The patient after full recovery from surgery, showing normal mobility and overall well-being.

sec.title: Exploratory laparotomy
sec.p: Five months after the removal of the supernumerary limb, an exploratory laparotomy was performed to further assess the internal anatomy. The delay in conducting this procedure was due to the owner’s unforeseen accident, which prevented earlier intervention. The laparotomy aimed to confirm the ultrasonographic findings and provide a more detailed evaluation of the urogenital structures.

During the procedure, we confirmed the presence of a single prostate with two distinct ureters originating from each bladder and converging at the prostate. Figure 7 illustrates the intraoperative findings, including the two bladders. However, due to surgical limitations and the need to minimize patient risk, detailed visualization of the ureters and kidneys in the pelvic region was not possible without creating a more extensive surgical wound. Figure 7.
Exploratory laparotomy findings, showing two bladders, though further detail regarding connections to ureters and kidneys is limited.

Given the satisfactory functionality of both bladders and the additional penis, these structures were preserved to avoid unnecessary complications. The surgical wound was then closed using standard procedures.

Additionally, an orchiectomy was performed via a pre-scrotal incision in each scrotal pouch to eliminate the potential risk of genetically transmitting this congenital anomaly. The patient recovered well, demonstrating excellent progress with no complications.

sec.title: Cytogenetic analysis
sec.p:
The chromosomal analysis showed a typically male karyotype 76, XY (Figure 8a). Frequency distribution analysis revealed a bimodal distribution of diploid numbers (Figure 9). Furthermore, the presence of a single cell with endoreduplication (Figure 10) was observed among 137 metaphases examined, suggesting an approximate incidence of 7.3 cells with endoreduplication per thousand cells examined. Figure 8.
Comparative karyotypes illustrate the normal 76,XY chromosomal configuration (a) and the putative aneuploid 75,XY chromosomal configuration (b). Figure 9.
Metaphase frequency distribution in the canine patient. Figure 10.
Endoreduplication phenomenon in tetraploid cells of the canine patient.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The present study describes a rare case of caudal duplication, a type of congenital abnormality, in a mixed-breed puppy. This case exemplifies the complex challenges posed by such anomalies, underscoring the need for a multifaceted approach involving clinical, surgical, and cytogenetic analysis.

Congenital deformities in veterinary medicine often exhibit a diverse range of phenotypic expressions, influenced by genetic, environmental, and multifactorial aetiologies [10]. These anomalies pose intricate challenges in practice, requiring meticulous documentation for a thorough comprehension. They arise during embryonic development, exhibiting diverse manifestations across species and being influenced by factors such as gestation period and environmental exposure. Notably, organogenesis during the initial third of gestation represents a critical phase highly vulnerable to these anomalies [11–13].

Polymelia is a congenital defect characterized by one or more supernumerary limbs. It can be further classified into notomelia (extra limbs on the back), cephalomelia (extra limbs on the head), thoracomelia (extra limbs on the chest), and pygomelia (extra limbs on the pelvis) [14,15]. Diphallia, on the other hand, is categorized into two types: complete (two penises) or partial (two separate glans) [16]. Until 2017, only five cases of diphallia in canines were documented in the literature [17–19], along with two cases of polymelia concurrent with diphallia [15].

In contrast, monocephalus dipygus is a condition characterized by the presence of supernumerary posterior limbs attached to the pelvic region. This anomaly may involve the duplication of pelvic organs originating from the urogenital sinus, including the bladder, most of the vagina, urethra, and external genitalia such as the vulva and penis. Dipygus manifests with two separate penises, each with two cavernous bodies and a urethra opening into its own bladder. Moreover, the bladders are perfectly separated, each receiving urine from a single kidney, and two scrotal sacs are present, each containing a single testis [20,21].

Considering the specific anatomical features observed in the case described, the diagnosis of monocephalus dipygus appears more appropriate than a case of concurrent polymelia and diphallia. This alignment with the established diagnostic criteria for monocephalus dipygus strengthens the validity of the diagnosis and underscores the importance of comprehensive documentation and analysis in understanding congenital anomalies in canines

Dogs typically have five toes on their front paws and four toes on their hind paws. Polydactyly, also known as canine preaxial polydactyly or CPP, involves the reappearance of the first toe, known as the hind dewclaw, on the pelvic limbs – a feature lost during canine evolutionary processes. This condition is caused by a mutation in the conserved intronic region of the LMBR1 gene and is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern across various breeds [22]. Given the lack of polydactyly in the familial history, it is reasonable to hypothesize that the anomaly observed in the puppy’s limb is better characterized as a duplication of the toes within the supernumerary limb. This implies a condition not previously identified, potentially linked to a diagnosis of monocephalus dipygus.

The surgical intervention, focused on the removal of the supernumerary limb, combined with postoperative care managed to restore the patient’s physical integrity, underscoring the effectiveness of surgical interventions to treat congenital anomalies, specifically those affecting supernumerary limbs. In addition to relieving the physical burden associated with the extra limb, this satisfactory result significantly improved the patient’s quality of life by returning movement and functionality.

In a cytogenetic context, the presence of cells with a normal diploid number of 78, XY (2n = 78), and cells with a reduced diploid number (2n = 77) at high frequencies suggests the coexistence of two cell lines, one normal and the other potentially caused by chromosomal abnormalities such as chromosomal loss (monosomy) or a Robertsonian translocation. However, this last hypothesis is not likely, due to the absence of any observed chromosome with a larger size than normal. It is worth noting that the analysis of aneuploid karyotypes (2n = 77), organized based on chromosomal size from largest to smallest, consistently reveals the absence of a chromosome always within the series ranging from pair 2 to pair 10. Despite this consistency, the great similarity in size and shape among these chromosomes makes it impossible to conclusively determine which specific pair exhibits the aneuploidy, nor whether the loss is always of the same pair or varies randomly. For illustrative purposes, this aneuploidy has been arbitrarily assigned to pair number 6 (Figure 7b). However, the identification remains subjective due to the challenge of clearly discerning them within the ordered series based on size. The lack of distinct markers, such as those provided by G-banding, further complicates the identification process, posing a challenge for their clear differentiation.

It is noteworthy that the variability observed in chromosome numbers among metaphase spreads indicates defective cell division and compromised chromosomal segregation, a condition known as chromosomal instability (CI). This genomic variability leads to changes in the number and/or structure of chromosomes, resulting in cells with an aberrant chromosome count. This chromosomal instability underscores the importance of the bimodal distribution observed in the diploid number frequency analysis, highlighting potential underlying genetic abnormalities in the canine patient [23,24].

Furthermore, the detection of endoreduplicated cells in the metaphase spreads of this canine patient (Figure 9) is significant, as endoreduplication involves DNA replication without subsequent mitosis [25,26]. This process has been associated with cellular strategies for gene amplification and overproduction of gene products [27,28], documented in both normal and cancerous cells in mammals [29]. Although the low frequency of observing endoreduplicated cells (1/137) in this study does not provide substantial evidence to establish a conclusive cause-effect relationship, their presence could be related to pathological conditions such as congenital malformations, developmental disorders, or predisposition to neoplasia. This finding could contribute to explaining the anomalies observed in the patient and highlights the importance of conducting detailed cytogenetic analyses. However, the possibility that the observed endoreduplication is due to alterations in the mitotic process, primarily influenced by specific cell culture conditions, cannot be overlooked.

Approximately 10% of congenital anomalies are attributed to environmental factors, while 25% are linked to genetic causes. However, the majority – around 65%—remain without a clearly identified cause [29]. The specific aetiology of non-hereditary malformations, such as the presence of supernumerary limbs, is still largely speculative and poorly understood. This complexity likely involves a combination of factors, including errors in cell migration, disruptions in cell differentiation, disturbances in chemical signalling, genetic influences, and notably, exposure to adverse environmental conditions during gestation [11,30–32]. Unfortunately, in this case, we lack direct evidence to determine the exact cause of the anomaly.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This case highlights the complexity of congenital malformations in animals and the necessity of integrating multiple scientific disciplines, such as veterinary medicine, genetics, embryology, and environmental science, to advance understanding and management strategies. The detailed morphological and karyotypic analysis provided valuable insights into the potential association of chromosomal abnormalities with physical deformities. Additionally, while the precise aetiology of the malformation remains unclear, the findings emphasize the critical need for further research into genetic and environmental factors, including potential teratogenic influences, to better elucidate the underlying causes.

The successful implementation of staged surgical interventions demonstrates the importance of a well-structured therapeutic approach, aiming to improve the patient’s quality of life. By documenting this case, we contribute to the limited veterinary literature on similar congenital conditions, underscoring the need for collaborative and interdisciplinary research efforts to deepen our understanding and enhance patient care.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).

sec.title: Ethical approval statement
sec.p: All procedures were carried out following good veterinary practices in which animals were handled. The study was approved by the institutional ethics committee with the corresponding protocol number (UTMACH-008-2024). Written informed consent was obtained from the animal owner for the participation of his/her pet in this research.
